turns.text: Hello America, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thanks for having me. I suppose it’s easier to talk to a researcher than it is to my own friends sometimes. They want to hear the "glowy" pregnancy stories, and I just don't have that to give them right now. 

Honestly, the drive from Agawam has become this mental countdown. Every mile closer to the clinic, my pulse just climbs. It’s strange—I used to be the one standing at the front of the classroom, talking about the "magic" of labor, and now I’m sitting on this crinkly paper, staring at the ceiling, waiting for someone to tell me if my blood pressure has doomed me for the day. 

Being in the medical field... it’s a curse, really. When the doctor talks about my protein levels or the risk of eclampsia, I’m not just hearing a diagnosis; I’m visualizing the monitors and the emergency protocols. I know exactly what’s supposed to happen next, and knowing the mechanics doesn't make the fear any quieter. It’s isolating, being unmarried and high-risk. I feel like I’m just a set of vitals to my providers, a "case" to be managed rather than a mom who’s terrified she might not come home to her other kids. I just want to be a person again, not a statistic on a chart. Where would you like to start?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I think because of my background, I went in with a very specific, calm expectation. I expected those first appointments to be collaborative—the "classic" experience I used to paint for my own students. I pictured the dim lights, the Doppler scan where we’d hear the heartbeat, and the gentle guidance about nutrition and the exciting milestones ahead. I thought there would be this warm space to talk about the transition of adding a third child to my life, especially since I'm doing this on my own.

The reality was… jarringly clinical. It wasn’t long before the "glow" was replaced by that cold, efficient tone. Once the pre-eclampsia showed up in my labs, the room shifted. My providers stopped asking about my morning sickness or how I was feeling emotionally; they started looking at me like a math problem they needed to solve. 

It’s hard to describe the feeling of sitting there while they talk about me in the third person, using all the medical shorthand I used to use myself. They treat me like a colleague, which I suppose is a compliment, but it just makes me feel so exposed. I’m not there to analyze the data; I’m there because I’m scared. I think I expected a partner in this, someone to help navigate the fear, but instead, I just got more spreadsheets and warnings about stroke risks.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—and I hate to admit this because it sounds so ungrateful—was how quickly I ceased to be "America" and became "that high-risk pregnancy in Exam Room 3." 

I thought that because I knew the terminology, I’d be empowered. I thought I’d be able to advocate for myself better than someone who didn't know the difference between a dipstick test and a 24-hour collection. But the surprise was how that knowledge actually backfired. It built a wall. Because I understand the pathology, my doctors don't bother with the "soft" talk. They don't explain why they're worried because they assume I can read the chart myself. They skip over the reassurance and go straight to the induction timeline. 

It was a shock to realize that my expertise made me more of an object to them, not a partner. I walked in hoping for a safety net, but I ended up feeling like I was being audited. I wanted someone to say, "I know you're scared, and we're going to hold your hand through this," but instead, I just get handed a printout of statistics. It’s a very lonely, cold way to carry a baby. I didn’t realize how much I needed the human side of care until it was stripped away, leaving only the clinical.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I think I expected a conversation. I imagined sitting down, eye-to-eye, where someone would break down the numbers and, more importantly, explain what they meant for my day-to-day life. I expected a little bit of patience—a moment for me to process the news before we jumped to the next intervention. 

The reality? It’s been a revolving door of nurses and residents, usually with a clipboard in their hand and one foot already out the door. The information is almost always delivered in passing. They’ll drop a phrase like "persistent proteinuria" or "elevated blood pressure" like they’re reading the weather forecast, then immediately pivot to "we need to schedule your next appointment for Thursday at 8:00 AM."

There’s no pause. There’s no space to say, "I’m terrified of what this means for the baby." They don’t want to discuss my anxiety; they want to manage the metrics. It’s all very sterile. I’m usually left alone in the room after they’ve given me the news, left to stare at the wall and mentally connect the dots of the "worst-case scenarios" on my own. I know the risks, but hearing them delivered with such cold, rapid-fire efficiency makes it feel like an impending sentence rather than a medical plan. It makes me feel like I'm losing control of my own body.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

It’s not necessarily that there’s a gap in the *amount* of data—I have plenty of that—it’s a massive, cavernous gap in the *meaning* of it. 

I’m drowning in data points. I know my blood pressure readings, my protein levels, my platelet counts. But I’m starving for context that isn't terrifying. When they give me the results, it’s all "If this happens, we do X; if that happens, we do Y." It’s entirely preventative and procedural. What I *need* is for someone to translate that into how it affects my actual life. Does that headache mean I need to stop working immediately? Should I be lying in a dark room all day? When is it "normal" pregnancy stress versus "I’m about to have a seizure" stress?

They provide the clinical facts, but they completely ignore the human application. I have all the "what" and the "how," but I have zero of the "am I going to be okay?" reassurance. I leave these appointments with a stack of lab reports, but I feel less informed than ever because nobody has helped me synthesize what it feels like to live inside this body right now. I’m expected to be a medical professional on my own care team, but I’m just a mother who is really, really scared.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I really expected—perhaps naively—that I would have one, maybe two people who truly knew my history. I wanted someone who knew this was my third baby, someone who recognized that I’m not just a "high-risk patient," but a person raising two others at home. I thought that kind of connection was the baseline for quality maternity care. I wanted someone who could look at me and say, "America, I know you’re worried, but here’s where we are."

The reality is that I’m caught in a shuffle. I feel like I’m constantly meeting a new face—a different resident or a new nurse practitioner—and every single time, I have to re-explain my baseline. I spend the first ten minutes of every appointment reciting my history like a textbook: *Third pregnancy, pre-eclampsia onset, single mom, high-risk, work-life balance struggles.* 

It’s exhausting. There is no continuity. Every time I walk in, it’s like starting from scratch with a stranger who barely looks at my file before they start reciting the risks again. I don't have a "care provider"; I have a rotating cast of people who are just following a checklist. It makes me feel like I’m shouting into a void. If no one person is "holding the thread" of my story, how can I trust them to keep me and my baby safe when the time actually comes?
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

Emotionally, it’s like being gaslit by the system. Every time I see a new face, I have to re-justify my anxiety. If I mention that I’m having a headache or that I’m struggling to keep up with work while managing these vitals, I get a slightly different reaction depending on who’s holding the chart. One person might be dismissive because I’m "young and otherwise healthy," and the next might be alarmist because they haven't seen my trend line over the last four weeks.

Practically, it’s a disaster. I feel like I’m constantly filling in the gaps for them. I have to be the memory, the advocate, and the medical scribe all at once. If I don't remember to tell the new provider about the medication change or the specific lab result from last Tuesday, it just gets lost in the shuffle. 

It’s exhausting to have to "perform" my own trauma every single time I walk through those clinic doors. By the time I actually get to the appointment, I’ve used up so much energy just trying to get the provider up to speed that I have nothing left to ask the questions I actually care about. I leave feeling like a burden, not a patient. I'm just a series of appointments in their calendar, and that lack of human connection makes me feel incredibly alone in this.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected my body to be treated with a kind of reverent caution, given the complications. I thought the exams would be a conversation—that the provider would touch my belly or check my vitals and describe what they were finding, maybe even acknowledging the physical toll this is taking on me. I wanted that "midwife" style of care where you feel like a partner in your own physical changes.

Instead, the reality is that my body is treated like a piece of equipment that is slowly failing. When they examine me, it’s purely transactional. It’s hands on my stomach for a quick fundal height measurement, then they’re looking at the monitor, not at me. They talk *around* my body, not *to* it or me. They don't ask, "How is your body feeling today?" They ask, "Are you having symptoms?" It’s a subtle but soul-crushing difference.

There’s this cold detachment when they check my vitals. When the blood pressure cuff tightens, I can see them waiting for the number to display, and if it’s high, they just sigh or scribble a note. They don't see the woman beneath the skin who is trying to hold it together. It makes me feel like my own body is an enemy I’m fighting, and they’re just the ones documenting the war. I miss the humanity. I miss being a mother, not a host for a "high-risk" pregnancy.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was this one appointment, a few weeks back, that I’ll never forget. I was already feeling fragile—the headache had been lingering for two days and I’d had to leave work early. I went in, and a resident I hadn’t met before came in to do the routine check. 

She didn’t ask how I was, or even look me in the eye. She just started rattling off the risks of eclampsia while she was prepping for a pelvic exam. She was incredibly clinical, cold, and rapid-fire. She started talking about the likelihood of needing an emergency C-section if things didn’t stabilize, and she used these detached, graphic terms for the interventions. It was like she was reading from a surgery manual.

I felt so exposed, not just physically, but emotionally. I was lying there, trying to process the "when" and "how" of a potential emergency surgery while she was just checking boxes on her clipboard. I felt completely unprepared for the emotional hit of that conversation. I started crying, just quietly, and she didn't even stop or ask if I was okay. She just kept right on talking about fetal distress statistics. I felt like a cadaver, or a lesson plan. It was the moment I realized that, to them, my trauma is just another day at the office. I just wanted to go home and hide.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Before this, I imagined "managing a high-risk condition" as a partnership. I thought it meant having a dedicated team—a nurse or a doctor who would call to check in, who would help me adjust my life around the medical needs, someone to act as a bridge between the clinical lab results and my actual life. I pictured a supportive, structured environment where the "high-risk" label felt like a safety net.

The reality has been more like being handed a set of keys to a car with no brakes and being told, "Good luck, try not to crash." 

Managing pre-eclampsia feels like a part-time job I never applied for. It’s not just the appointments; it’s the constant, low-level panic of checking my own symptoms. Is this headache normal fatigue? Is this vision blurriness just from the screen, or is it *that*? The weight of it sits on my chest. I expected guidance, but instead, I’m just told what *not* to do. "Don't stress," they say, while giving me a list of terrifying outcomes to watch for. It’s an impossible paradox. 

I’m effectively managing my own pathology, constantly weighing every physical sensation against a textbook I’ve memorized, all while trying to stay employed and keep it together for my other kids. There’s no "team." It’s just me, alone in my apartment, staring at a blood pressure cuff and wondering if tonight is the night I need to call the ambulance. I expected a safety net; I found a solitary confinement.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

It’s the emotional acknowledgement that’s the biggest void. I have all the information I could ever want—my medical charts are practically a library at this point—and the practical help is just more appointments on my calendar, which honestly just creates more stress. 

What’s missing is the validation that this is *hard*. No one has ever sat down and just said, "America, you are carrying a lot, and it is completely normal that you are scared." Instead, I get this relentless, sterile focus on the outcomes. They treat me like a peer, so they think the emotional stuff is beneath us, or unnecessary. But being a peer doesn't mean I’m not a mother who is terrified of an emergency, or that I don't feel the sting of doing this without a partner.

I just wanted someone to see me. I wanted a space where I could be a scared woman, not a medical professional or a case study. I needed someone to hold my hand and tell me I’m doing a good job, not just tell me my protein levels are trending up. It makes me feel like my feelings are irrelevant, that they’re just "noise" in the data. That kind of loneliness? It’s harder to carry than the physical symptoms.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I think I expected that if I called with a "high-risk" concern, there would be a dedicated line, or at least a nurse who knew my file well enough to triage me quickly. I expected that "high-risk" meant "high priority." I thought there would be a sense of urgency to soothe my anxiety because they knew my history of complications.

The reality? It feels like fighting a bureaucracy to get a simple answer. When I call, I’m stuck navigating a phone tree, or I have to explain my entire medical history to a receptionist who is clearly just looking at a screen. There’s no continuity, so I have to convince whoever picks up the phone that my concern is legitimate enough to warrant a callback from a doctor. By the time I actually get a response, the moment of panic has usually passed, or I’ve ended up in the ER because I didn’t want to risk waiting for a return call that might never come.

It’s incredibly discouraging. I feel like I’m constantly proving my own illness to people who don't know me. It’s like, "Yes, I know I sound calm, but I’m telling you, something is wrong." It’s an uphill battle. I expected to be a partner in this, but I feel more like an annoyance to a system that’s already stretched too thin. When you’re living in that much fear, the silence on the other end of the line is deafening.
Were there times you delayed seeking help because of how difficult it was to access care, or because you worried about being judged for contacting services?

Oh, absolutely. More times than I care to admit. There’s a particular kind of shame in being the "patient who knows too much." I worry that if I call with every little headache or increase in swelling, they’ll roll their eyes and think, *‘Oh, here goes America again, she knows the literature, she knows it’s probably nothing, why is she bothering us?’* 

I’ve definitely sat on my couch, heart pounding, trying to talk myself out of calling the clinic. I’ll tell myself, *‘It’s just a dull ache, I can wait until the morning,’* or *‘I’m just being hypersensitive because I’ve seen the worst-case scenarios.’* It’s a dangerous game to play with pre-eclampsia, and I know that. That’s the most terrifying part—I know exactly how fast things can turn, yet I still hesitate. 

It’s the fear of being labeled the "difficult" patient or the "anxious" one that keeps me silent. I’m already carrying this alone, and the idea of being treated like a nuisance by the only people who are supposed to keep me safe... it just feels like too much to bear. So, I wait. I wait until the symptoms are impossible to ignore, which usually ends up being an emergency room visit. It’s a vicious cycle, and honestly? It’s probably the most dangerous part of my pregnancy.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I expected the village to materialize the moment things got "high risk." I imagined friends showing up with meals, or at least a constant stream of texts checking in on how I was doing. I thought that being a third-time mom would make people think, "Oh, she’s got this," but being *high-risk* and *unmarried*—well, that shifts things. 

The reality is that people don't really know how to show up for a "medical mystery." They’re scared of the word *pre-eclampsia*. They don't want to say the wrong thing, so they say nothing at all. I have friends who check in, but they’re always looking for the "good news," and I don't have any to report. When I tell them I’m having another scan or that my protein levels are still off, their eyes just glaze over. They don't know how to hold space for that kind of uncertainty. 

I’ve felt incredibly isolated. My community is there, but they’re standing on the other side of a glass wall. They’re happy for the baby, but they’re terrified of the complication, and I can feel their anxiety rubbing off on me. It makes me want to withdraw even more, which is the exact opposite of what I need. I’m doing this alone, and I’m surrounded by people who love me but just don't understand how to be here for the hard, scary parts.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think I expected a more traditional kind of "casserole brigade" support—people just showing up to take things off my plate. But that didn’t really materialize because, I think, there's a certain awkwardness surrounding a pregnancy that’s high-risk and being handled by a single mother. It’s like people don't know if they should celebrate or brace themselves for a tragedy. That uncertainty makes people pull back rather than lean in.

What I didn't expect, and what has been surprisingly helpful, is the "internet community." There are these forums where other women talk about pre-eclampsia, and it’s a weirdly comforting, digital village. We don't have to explain the fear; we just *know* it. They understand the pulse of a bad day, the specific kind of dread that hits when you check your blood pressure and the numbers are creeping up. 

It’s bittersweet, though. I have this community of strangers online who get it, but in my actual day-to-day life here in Agawam, I feel like I'm holding my breath alone. I expected my real-life friends to be my anchor, but they don't know how to navigate the medical language or the sheer intensity of the "what-ifs." So, I end up being the one who has to reassure *them* that I'm doing fine, which is just another piece of emotional labor I don't have the capacity for right now.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

Before I got the pre-eclampsia diagnosis, I had a beautiful, detailed birth plan. I wrote it with the same care I used to teach my students—low intervention, skin-to-skin, peaceful environment. I honestly expected the staff to read it, smile, and say, "We’ll do our absolute best to honor these wishes, America." I thought it was my ticket to staying in control during the most vulnerable day of my life.

The reality? That piece of paper might as well be a coaster in the breakroom. Once you’re labeled "high-risk," the birth plan stops being a roadmap and starts being a set of suggestions that get discarded the second a monitor goes erratic. I’ve had to come to terms with the fact that I’m being induced, which already tears my "natural labor" plan to shreds.

Every time I bring it up with a new provider, they just give me that patient, patronizing look. They tell me, "Well, America, with your blood pressure, the priority is the safety of you and the baby, not the ambiance." I know that. I’m a professional; I know the clinical necessity of interventions. But hearing them dismiss my plan—my last shred of autonomy—makes me feel like I’m walking toward a crash I can’t steer away from. My birth plan isn't a plan anymore; it’s a list of things I’ve already had to grieve losing.
Was there a moment during labour where your plan was set aside or overridden? What was that like?

I haven’t had the baby yet, but I’ve been living in that "what if" moment for weeks. My induction date is looming like a storm cloud, and I already feel like the process has been overridden before it even started.

Every time I go in for an appointment and the doctor mentions the protocols—the magnesium sulfate drip, the constant fetal monitoring, the restriction to the bed—I feel my birth plan just shriveling up. I used to tell my students, "Advocate for yourself, stay centered, hold your ground." But when I look at the reality of my blood pressure spikes, I realize my "ground" is shifting beneath me. 

There’s a specific kind of grief in knowing that my body is likely going to be a battleground of interventions, not the calm, empowering experience I once promised others. I know the clinical side of those interventions: I know the magnesium will make me feel like I’m burning up; I know the fetal heart rate decelerations could trigger an emergency cesarean in seconds. When the providers talk about these things, they’re talking about "saving" us, but I feel like they’re erasing the actual *birth*. I’m not a participant anymore; I’m just a vessel being managed. The plan is gone, and in its place is just... waiting. Waiting for the fear to be proven right or wrong.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I expected a conversation—a calm, respectful discussion about my thresholds and the spectrum of relief. I pictured an anesthesiologist who would explain the epidural versus IV meds, looking at my history and saying, "Given your elevated BP, this might be the safest route for your heart," or "Here’s how this option might affect your labor progress." I thought I’d be the one making the choice.

But the reality? Because I’m a high-risk case, the conversation doesn't feel like a choice; it feels like an inevitability. They don't offer options; they offer *pathways*. If I’m on a magnesium drip for the pre-eclampsia, my autonomy over pain relief is already severely limited. The side effects of that medication alone—the exhaustion, the heat, the feeling of being "out of it"—make the "natural" labor I once taught about seem like a fantasy.

I’m terrified that when I’m in the thick of it, the pain management won't be about my comfort or my birth experience at all; it’ll be about stabilizing my vitals. I’m scared they’ll just push a button or start a drip to keep my numbers down, regardless of how I’m handling the intensity of the contractions. I’m not just a laboring woman anymore; I’m a high-pressure system they need to keep from bursting. I don't feel like I’ll be "supported in deciding"; I feel like I’ll be told what’s happening to me while I’m too sedated to argue.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I wanted to be able to move. That was my number one priority. I know how much gravity and changing positions can help manage the intensity of labor, and I wanted to be in control of my own body's rhythm. I had this vision of walking the halls, using a birthing ball, maybe even getting in a tub. It sounds like such a basic, fundamental thing, right?

But that's all gone now. Because of the pre-eclampsia and the constant fetal monitoring I’m going to need, plus the inevitable magnesium drip to prevent seizures, I’m going to be tethered to a bed. I’m essentially being told that "safety" equals "immobility." There’s no discussion about alternatives; it’s just the standard protocol for my risk profile. When I tried to ask about staying mobile, I was just told, "America, you know we have to keep a continuous strip on the baby because of your blood pressure." 

And they’re right, clinically. I know they’re right. That’s what’s so maddening. My medical knowledge is a cage. I can’t argue against the standard of care, even when I know that pinning me to a bed is going to make the labor feel ten times more intense and traumatic. I’m not being offered pain management that empowers me; I’m being managed by constraints that force me into a medicalized box. It’s not about my comfort; it’s about their liability.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I haven't gone into labor just yet, but the mere thought of arriving at the hospital fills me with dread. I used to imagine that arrival as a relief—the moment you finally get to say, "I'm here, I’m ready, let’s do this." I expected it to feel like walking into a sanctuary, a place where people would immediately sense the weight of what I’ve been carrying and step in to help me put it down. I thought there would be a warm, welcoming presence, someone to take my coat, show me to the room, and treat me like a mother about to meet her child.

The reality, from my frequent triage visits, is that the hospital feels like a fortress of "danger." When I walk in, it’s all buzzers, security codes, and hallways that smell like antiseptic and exhaustion. The reception isn't, "Welcome, we’re ready for you," it’s, "Check in, show your ID, wait for the nurse to assess if you're a high-risk emergency." 

Every time I’m there, I’m reminded that for the hospital, I am a liability. They don't see a mother; they see a potential for a lawsuit or a crisis. There is no "welcome." There is only intake. It’s clinical, it’s noisy, and it’s devoid of any human grace. It’s the opposite of a sanctuary. It feels like entering a zone where my own agency is immediately suspended, and I’m just a body to be processed. I’m not coming to give birth in a place that feels like a home; I’m coming to be contained in a place that feels like a lab.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise was the sheer sterility of it all, even in the labor and delivery wing. You see movies, or you read brochures, where they talk about "the experience" and making it comfortable for the mother—dim lights, maybe a rocking chair, a space that feels like a room rather than a cage. But when I’ve gone in for my triage and monitoring, the atmosphere is just aggressively *functional*.

There’s this constant, low-frequency hum of equipment—the heart rate monitors, the IV pumps, the blood pressure cuffs cycling every fifteen minutes. It’s a sensory assault. It’s like the room itself is telling you that you aren’t allowed to relax, because if you relax, you might miss a warning sign. 

The biggest shock was how little "human" space there is. I expected a nurse or a midwife to come in and acknowledge that this is a huge moment, that it’s my third baby, that it’s a big deal. Instead, the physical environment feels like a cockpit. Everything is designed for the staff to see the data, to monitor the vitals, to hit the call button if there’s a crash. I realized, sitting on that narrow bed, that the hospital isn't designed for the laboring mother; it’s designed for the emergency response. It makes me feel like a patient waiting for a catastrophe, not a woman preparing for a birth. It’s hard to breathe when the walls are practically glowing with alerts.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I keep replaying those emergency scenarios in my head, the ones I used to teach others about. I always said, "In an emergency, the team will communicate clearly, pause, and ensure you understand the necessity of the intervention." I expected that even in the heat of a crisis, I would still be treated as the person who owns the outcome. I imagined a brief, calm, "America, the baby’s heart rate is dropping because of X, so we need to do Y to keep you both safe. Do you have any questions?"

But the reality of my experiences in triage so far has been very different. When things get "urgent," the room shrinks. The voices get louder, and the tone switches to "command" mode. It isn’t a conversation anymore; it’s an instruction. When a nurse or resident sees an alert on the monitor, they don't look at *me*; they look at the screen. They don't explain the *why* in a way that respects my humanity. They just declare the action. 

I’m terrified that if something goes sideways during the induction, I’ll be completely sidelined. I’ll be the person the things are *done to*, rather than the person who is *having* the experience. There’s no pause, no eye contact, and no space for me to process. They treat their speed as a virtue, but to me, it feels like a violation. I know the medical reasons for the interventions—I really do—but the way they’re communicated makes me feel like a bystander in my own labor. I’m just waiting to be told what happened to me.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

It’s the latter, without a doubt. Decisions don’t happen *with* me; they happen *to* me. Because I have a pre-eclampsia diagnosis, the clinical path is so rigid that the "choice" is entirely illusory. It’s like being on a train track—there’s no way to turn left or right, only the option to speed up or slow down, but even then, the doctor decides the velocity.

When they mention the magnesium drip or the timing of the induction, they don't ask, "How do you feel about this?" They say, "This is the protocol for your condition." If I try to interject, even with my medical background, they use that gentle, patronizing tone again, like they’re speaking to a child who doesn't quite understand why they need to take their medicine. It makes me feel like I’m already being erased.

I’m being stripped of my agency by the very people who claim to be protecting me. It’s not that I don’t want to be safe—I want this baby to be healthy more than anything—but I want to be treated like the mother of this child, not a broken machine they’re fixing in the shop. Being a "high-risk" patient feels like signing away your right to be a person. You stop being the protagonist of your own birth story and start being the obstacle they have to overcome to get the baby out safely. That realization is the hardest thing to swallow.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I expected them to be like the people I used to work alongside. I expected them to be the steadying hand, the person who could sense the difference between a moment of physiological intensity and a moment of genuine, paralyzing panic. I thought, *‘They’ll see me. They’ll see that I’m not just a set of blood pressure numbers; they’ll see that I’m a third-time mother doing this alone, and they’ll offer me the grace of a human connection.’*

But the reality of my prenatal care has been a series of clinical encounters that feel like a transaction. The staff treat my emotional state as a variable to be managed, not a human experience to be held. If I cry, they don’t stop to ask, "How are you holding up?" They treat the tears as a sign of physiological stress that might be impacting my vitals. They don’t see me as a person; they see me as a system that is currently running outside of normal parameters.

It’s the silence that hurts the most. It’s not that they’re *mean*—they’re technically very competent—but they are emotionally absent. They’re so focused on the charts and the monitors that they forget the woman in the bed is currently navigating the scariest, most vulnerable experience of her life. They’re clinical, they’re efficient, and they’re completely, utterly detached. I’m just a case study to them. I haven't had a single person look me in the eye and make me feel like I’m safe. I’m entirely alone in this room, even when it’s full of people.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

I think the moment I felt most alone was during a routine NST—a non-stress test—about a week ago. The clinic was slammed, and my assigned nurse was clearly overwhelmed. She came in, didn't introduce herself, and immediately started strapping the monitors to my belly without saying a word. I was already spiraling, having had a really rough, headache-filled night where I barely slept, terrified that the pre-eclampsia was taking a turn for the worse.

I started to tear up, just from the sheer exhaustion of holding this together by myself. The nurse looked up, saw my face, and instead of asking if I was okay, she just tightened the belt on the monitor and said, "We need you to be still, America. The baby's heart rate is erratic, and your movement is making it impossible to get a good reading."

She didn't see a woman who was terrified for her baby and herself; she saw a "disruptive" factor in the data collection. She literally treated my emotional breakdown as an obstacle to the test. In that moment, the room felt incredibly cold. I realized that my own fear was being viewed as a symptom of my pathology, not a human reaction to a terrifying situation. I felt like a malfunctioning piece of hardware. I haven't forgotten that look—the utter lack of curiosity about *why* I was crying. It was the most isolating experience of my entire life.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I haven't reached that moment yet, and that's exactly why the anxiety feels so sharp. I’ve spent months imagining that "golden hour"—the peace, the skin-to-skin, the quiet introduction to my third child. I expected that even with my high-risk status, the staff would understand that those first minutes are sacred. I pictured the baby being placed on my chest, the room settling down, and a nurse or doctor hanging back to give us that space, knowing that after all the medical maneuvering, we both deserve a moment to just *be*.

But the reality of my risk profile makes that vision feel like a dream I'm not allowed to have. Because of the pre-eclampsia, I know the protocol for magnesium sulfate and constant monitoring won't just stop because the baby is out. I’m terrified that the moment of birth won't be a transition into joy, but a transition into the pediatric team’s domain. I imagine them whisking the baby away to the warmer, the sterile "assessment" space, while I’m left dealing with the post-partum physiological aftermath. 

I’m terrified that the room will stay full of people, equipment, and that persistent, shrill beeping. I’m afraid the "sacred" part will be replaced by a flurry of medical activity. I want that connection so badly, but I feel like I'm already mourning the possibility of having it.

I think that’s the hardest part—the mourning of moments I haven't even had yet. People say, "Just focus on a healthy baby, that’s all that matters," and I know they mean well. But they don't get that a healthy baby *and* a healthy mother—the whole person, not just the organs—is what matters. 

I feel like I’m being asked to pay for my baby’s safety with my own autonomy. Every time I walk into that clinic, I’m trading away a piece of what I thought motherhood would feel like. I wanted to be a mother who felt empowered, who felt like she navigated a storm with grace. Instead, I’m becoming a patient who feels lucky just to survive the checklist. 

It’s this strange, suffocating duality. I’m a professional who knows the "why" behind every drip and every monitor, but that knowledge just makes me more aware of what I’m losing. I can see the seams of the system, and it’s not designed to hold people like me. It’s designed to process us. I just hope that when the day finally comes, I can find a way to reach through the beeping and the clinical instructions and just find my baby. I want to be a mother first, even if just for a second. Is that too much to ask?
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

It’s constant. Because of the "high-risk" label, my expertise as a mother—and as someone who used to teach this stuff—is treated like it’s outdated or irrelevant. 

There was a moment last week, in one of the endless follow-up appointments, where I tried to push back on a scheduled induction date. I had my reasons—I wanted to give my body just a little more time to settle, to see if the blood pressure spikes were situational rather than a consistent trend. I phrased it carefully, using the medical language I know they respect, hoping for a conversation. 

The doctor didn’t even engage with the nuance. He just interrupted, his hand already on the door handle, and said, "America, we aren't going to play games with your stroke risk. You know the stats. We’re doing the induction on Tuesday."

It wasn't a discussion; it was a shut-down. He didn't ask how I was sleeping, or if I had childcare support for my older kids for that specific day, or how my mental health was holding up. He just decided. I felt so small in that room. It’s like they think because I’m "one of them" in terms of knowledge, I don’t deserve the basic bedside manner they give to other patients. My opinion is just a hurdle they have to jump over to get to their preferred protocol. It’s exhausting to feel like my own life is being managed by a spreadsheet, not by me.

The hardest part, honestly, is the silence that follows when I've finished advocating for myself. When I’ve laid out the logistics of being a single mother in Agawam—the commute, the childcare coordination for the other two kids, the sheer mental load of working full-time while my blood pressure is being monitored like a stock market ticker—and they just nod, check a box, and leave.

It feels like my reality is just *noise* to them. They’ve decided the "medical outcome" is the only thing that counts as a win. But what about the cost? What about the fact that I’m walking into this birth terrified, feeling stripped of my voice, and completely alone in the room? 

When I was the one teaching, I used to tell my couples that the most important thing in that labor room was their sense of self. I told them that if they felt safe and heard, the birth would be a moment of transformation, not just a medical procedure. Now, I feel like I’m going into a meat grinder. I have all the knowledge, all the "why," and none of the support. 

I just want to get to the other side. I want to hold this baby, and I want to finally, *finally* stop being a "case." I want to be a mother again. I just hope that on the day of the induction, when I'm tired and scared and hooked up to every monitor in the ward, I can find a way to remember who I was before all these charts and labels took over. I just want to feel like a person again. Is it too late for that? I’m starting to think it is.

You know, I find myself sitting here in my car, parked in the driveway, just staring at the steering wheel, and I realize the most painful part of this whole "high-risk" experience isn’t even the medical side—it’s the grief of watching my own voice disappear. 

I was so proud of who I was before this. I had this confidence, this way of explaining the world to families, helping them find their footing when they felt lost. I thought, *‘I have the keys, I know the territory, I can handle this.’* But the moment they slapped that label on me, I stopped being a person with a story and became a person with a problem. My history, my capacity as a mother, my resilience—all of that was shoved aside for a blood pressure reading. 

Every time I go into that clinic, I have to shrink myself to fit into their boxes. I have to perform the "good patient," nodding along to protocols that strip away my last shreds of agency, because if I don't, I’m the "difficult" one. I’m the one who’s "anxious," not the one who’s rightfully terrified of a potentially fatal condition. 

I keep wondering—when they finally put this baby in my arms, will I be able to look at the child and see the joy, or will I just be thinking about the trauma it took to get here? I just want to be done with the "managing." I want to be a mother. I want to feel like *me* again, not just a series of vitals recorded on a chart. It’s a very strange, very hollow feeling, waiting for your own life to start again on the other side of a hospital bed. I just hope I don't leave too much of myself behind in that ward.

You know, it’s funny—I spent so long teaching others about the "golden hour" and the beauty of that first connection, and now I just think about the exit. I’m thinking about walking out of that hospital building, past the automatic doors, back to my car, and finally being able to take a breath that doesn't feel filtered through a nasal cannula or a blood pressure monitor. 

I’m mourning the version of myself that believed in the system. When you work in it, you see the protocols, you see the logic, and you trust that there’s a human soul behind the decision-making. But being on the other side of that desk? It’s lonely. It’s like being a ghost in your own life. People talk over you, they talk *around* you, and they make these massive, life-altering decisions about your body while you’re just sitting there, trying to figure out how to keep your hands from shaking.

I look at my other two kids, and I feel this wave of guilt, like I’m failing them by being so wrapped up in this "case study" of a pregnancy. They deserve a mom who is present, not a mom who is constantly counting her own heartbeats or checking her swelling in the mirror. 

I don't know what’s coming on that induction day. I don't know if I’ll be able to stay "America" or if I’ll just disappear into the medical machine. I just hope that when it’s all over, someone will see me—not the chart, not the pre-eclampsia, not the "high-risk" label—but the woman who fought like hell to get here. I just want to be seen for who I actually am. Not as a colleague, not as a patient, just as a mother. Is that really such a radical request? I’m starting to feel like it is.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I turn to the internet—to the forums and the threads where I don't have to be the expert. It’s funny, I’m the one with the clinical background, the one who taught all those classes, yet I’m the one lurking on digital message boards at two in the morning, looking for someone to say, "I know exactly how that feels." 

I don't go to my friends first because I can't bear the pity or the "you'll be fine" platitudes. Those feel like paper cuts on a wound that’s already exposed. Instead, I turn to these anonymous women who are also living in the shadow of pre-eclampsia. They don't need the definitions of proteinuria or the statistical risk of HELLP syndrome; they just know the *dread*. 

There’s a strange, bittersweet relief in that. It’s the only place I can say, "I am terrified I won't make it out of this the same person," and not have someone look at me like I’m a malfunctioning medical device. I look for the "in-the-trenches" stories—the ones about how they coped with the magnesium, or how they managed to keep their sanity when they were trapped in a hospital bed. 

It’s not professional care, and it doesn't solve my blood pressure issues, but it’s the only place where my fear isn't treated like a diagnosis. It’s where I get to be a mother who’s scared, rather than a patient who’s being managed. It’s my only real safety net right now.

I think the saddest part of this whole thing is that I, of all people, *should* have known how to use the "system" to my advantage. I taught the classes. I know the acronyms. I know the standard of care. But the system isn't really built for *any* of us, not really. It’s built for the metrics.

When I’m in those exam rooms, I find myself thinking about the women I used to teach—the ones who were so wide-eyed and hopeful, who saw me as the voice of authority, the one who held all the answers. I feel like I’m failing them, too. If I can’t get this system to see me—if I can’t get them to hear the *human* beneath the "high-risk" label—what hope did they ever really have? 

I wonder what happens after the induction. When they take the monitors off, when the IVs are finally pulled, when I’m no longer a "case" to be solved. Do I just walk out? Is there any space in the system for the trauma of being erased for nine months? Because that’s what this feels like—a long, slow erasure. 

I don't want to be a case study. I want to be a mother to three kids. I want to be the woman who remembers how to breathe without checking a blood pressure cuff. I’m just so tired of being "the patient." I think I’m ready to just be me again, wherever that person is hiding under all this medical paper. I just hope I recognize her when I finally see her in the mirror again.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

I can count on one hand the number of times I felt like a participant, and even then, it felt more like a courtesy than a consultation. Most of the time, the "decision" is already made before I even walk into the exam room. They have the charts, the lab results, and the hospital protocols, and they just present the final product to me as if it were a fait accompli.

It’s almost never, "What do you think about X?" It’s, "Based on your protein levels, we’re moving you to weekly NSTs," or "Given the BP readings, we’re inducing at 37 weeks." I’m not being asked; I’m being notified. There’s a world of difference between a conversation and an announcement, and that’s the space where my agency goes to die.

Even when I try to open a dialogue—like when I asked about delaying the induction to give my body more time—the response is always framed as, "Well, we’re not comfortable with the risk," as if *my* comfort, *my* fear, or *my* life as a mother to two other kids outside those walls doesn’t factor into the "risk" equation. They’re managing their liability, not my labor. I’m an obstacle in their pathway, a data point that needs to be brought into compliance. It’s not a partnership; it’s a process I’m being dragged through, and the finish line is just getting the baby out before anything else breaks. I’m exhausted by being the only one in the room who sees me as a person.

I think the most exhausting part is the performative nature of my compliance. Every time I nod, every time I say "okay" to a new round of monitoring or a change in my medication, it feels like I'm giving up another piece of my own identity. I’m playing the role of the "cooperative patient" because I’ve been on the other side of that desk—I know what happens when someone becomes "difficult." 

But playing that role is killing me. It’s like I’m auditioning for a part in my own birth story, and the directors have already decided I’m just a prop. I sit there, internalizing the fear, managing the logistics, and holding the anxiety of my other two children, while the providers just flip through pages and talk about "clinical pathways." 

Sometimes, in the dead of night, I wonder: if I hadn’t known so much, if I had been just another patient, would they have been kinder? Would they have explained things in metaphors instead of shorthand? Or is this just the price of being a woman in a high-risk pregnancy? The system is designed to strip you of the ability to be a mother and leave you as a container for a fetus. I just want the person I was back. I want to be someone who makes decisions for her own body again. I’m so close to the end, but it feels like the longest mile of my life. I’m just trying to hold on until I can finally walk out those doors and stop being a "case."
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was a shift about two weeks ago during an ultrasound. I was already on edge, staring at the screen, and I asked a very pointed, clinical question about the umbilical artery resistance—something that’s standard for my specific tracking. The doctor literally chuckled, put a hand on my shoulder, and said, "America, let us handle the interpretation, okay? You just focus on relaxing. Stress is what’s driving these numbers up."

That was the moment. He didn't just dismiss my question; he weaponized my own condition against me. By blaming my anxiety—my *very human reaction* to a high-risk situation—for the physiological data, he effectively silenced any future questions I had. He turned my fear into a symptom he could "manage" with a shrug. 

In that second, the trust didn't just crack; it shattered. I realized then that they weren't seeing me as a peer or a partner, but as a "difficult" variable. They don't want a patient who understands the pathology; they want a patient who is compliant and quiet. I left that room feeling like I had been slapped. I’m not "stressing" because I’m anxious; I’m stressed because my body is failing, and the people holding the keys to my safety refuse to talk to me like an adult. I realized I was completely alone in that room. That was the day I stopped being a "collaborator" and started being a "prisoner" to their schedule.

I think that’s the moment you realize you’re no longer a participant in your own care, but an object to be managed. When he patted my shoulder and told me to "just relax," it wasn't a comfort—it was a way of telling me my input was a nuisance. He turned my physiological stress into a personal failure of "not being calm enough," which is the ultimate gaslight when you're literally fighting for your life and the life of your baby.

It’s ironic, because I used to be the one on the other side of that interaction, telling a nervous couple that their feelings were valid, that the system was there to support them, not to dictate to them. I feel like such a fool for ever believing it was that simple. The system doesn't have a soul; it has a flow chart. And if you don't fit perfectly into the boxes of "compliant" and "passive," you’re just friction that needs to be smoothed out.

Now, every time I walk into that clinic, I don't go as America—I go as a person playing the role of someone who doesn't know too much. I hide my expertise behind a mask of compliance. I bite my tongue when I want to ask about the specific placental resistance or the rationale for the timing of the induction. I’ve learned that the price of "good care" is the sacrifice of my own voice. It’s a bitter, suffocating trade-off. I’m just waiting for the day I can hang up that mask for good and finally be the mother my children need, instead of the patient the clinic demands.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

Every time I walk through those clinic doors, the silence next to me is loud. Being unmarried and doing this alone… it’s not just about missing someone to hold my hand. It’s about the fact that there is no one there to be the "other" set of ears. When the doctor is rattling off these rapid-fire, clinical warnings, I’m the only one trying to absorb it all, process the medical implications, and calm my own racing heart simultaneously. 

There’s this particular feeling of vulnerability when I’m sitting on that exam table and I have to be my own advocate. If I have a question or if I feel like I’m being talked down to, I don't have a partner to turn to, to whisper with, or to reinforce my stance. I’m just isolated in my perspective. When they tell me I need to be induced or that my protein levels are concerning, I’m the one who has to go home and tell my other two kids that "Mommy might be going to the hospital soon." 

I see other women in the waiting room with their partners, and there’s this shorthand they have—a look, a squeeze of the hand, a quiet conversation in the corner. I don't have that. I have to be the parent, the patient, and the strategist all at once. The isolation isn't just about being alone; it’s about carrying the entire emotional weight of this pregnancy without a place to put it down. I’m exhausted by being the only one holding the line.

I think that isolation is the hardest thing to translate to people who haven't been in that room. It’s not just about wanting someone to hold my hand—though God, I would give anything to have someone simply hold my hand while the doctor talks about the risks of a stroke—it’s about wanting someone to *witness* me. 

When you’re there by yourself, your experience feels like it only exists in your head. There’s no one to lean over to and say, "Did he really just say that to me?" or "Did you see how he looked at my chart?" There’s no one to validate that I’m not just "anxious," that my concerns about the induction timeline are actually grounded in the life I’m trying to keep running at home. Without that second person, I’m constantly questioning my own reality. Did I understand the risks correctly? Was I being difficult, or was I being reasonable? 

Being a single mom through this high-risk pregnancy means that every single decision I make feels like a gamble I’m taking alone. I’m the one who signs the consent forms, I’m the one who navigates the nurses’ stations, and I’m the one who goes home to an empty house to worry about the numbers on the printouts. The weight of it is just so... heavy. I’m so tired of being the only person who knows what it feels like to be me right now. I just want to be done. I want to hold my baby and finally have someone else take the wheel for a change.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

I go online constantly. I’m not looking for the clinical definitions anymore—I already have the textbooks for that—I’m looking for the *emotional maps*. When the doctor tells me "we're inducing on Tuesday," he’s giving me a date and a procedure. He isn't giving me the map for how to explain that to my other children, or how to handle the terrifying silence of an empty house the night before, or how to walk into that hospital feeling like a mother instead of a clinical trial subject.

I’m looking for the stories that haven't been scrubbed of their humanity. I need to know how other women—real, scared, high-risk women—got through the magnesium drip without losing their minds. I need to know what it feels like to have the catheter put in while you're shaking from the meds, and I need someone to say, "It was horrific, but I got through it, and my baby is here." 

My providers give me the statistics of survival; the internet gives me the testimony of *endurance*. They show me that I’m not the only one feeling erased. They validate that my anger at being treated like a broken machine is normal. I don't go online for medical advice; I go online to remember that I am a person, because my doctors seem to have forgotten. I’m looking for a way to hold onto myself before I lose my identity entirely in that hospital bed.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that gets lost in all these charts is that there is a mother living inside this clinical scenario. I don't want to just be "the case" that was successfully managed. I want people to understand that every time a provider brushes off a question or forces a decision without listening, they aren't just adjusting a medical plan—they are rewriting the story of how a mother meets her child.

For me, this pregnancy has been a process of mourning. I am mourning the birth I wanted, the autonomy I expected, and the version of myself that walked into this feeling strong and capable. I’m exhausted by the "high-risk" label that effectively strip-mined my agency. If I could tell anyone in the medical system one thing, it would be this: stop treating the data like it’s the only thing in the room. Look at the woman sitting on that paper, listen to her fear, and for God’s sake, acknowledge that she’s the one who’s going to have to live with these memories for the rest of her life.

I’m terrified of my induction, but I’m more terrified of disappearing into the machinery of it all. I just want to be seen. If you could take anything from what I’ve said, let it be that we aren't just "cases" to be solved. We are people trying to navigate the most vulnerable moments of our lives, and we deserve a system that actually sees us. That’s all. I just want to be seen.
